summary: Stancil argues that secondary sales are not inherently unethical and that VCs’ moral posturing is inconsistent with how they actually reward founders who make money, even after failures. The real fairness issue isn’t whether founders sell, but whether employees—who bear the most concentrated risk—get transparency and a chance to judge or participate.
money_quotes: Because, for all of the hand-wringing on Sand Hill Road about this stuff, Silicon Valley is really just about making money.
Investors don't invest in nice people; they invest in monetizable people.
It is not enough for a founder to merely want their startup to succeed; they have to need it to succeed.
Secondary sales are fine, so long as everyone in the company is told about them.
key_insight: Secondary sales should be acceptable, but employees—not VCs—should be the ones to judge their fairness because they bear the most concentrated risk.
